posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked:
*edited based on @J_Rock talking some sense into me, ha!

Just received the letter for the 2020 vintage release. I was already on the fence as prices keep creeping up…but the 2020 vintage gives me even greater pause. Will have to see the release notes and of course the $$$

'Thank you for your patience in the 2020 release for Ultramarine. Because of your continued support we are offering you and earlier allocation, two days before the main release.

We will be holding only one release this year. And while we will guarantee the allocations of the 2020 vintage wine, we will not guarantee the allocations of magnums or late disgorged wines.

The 2020 vintage in California was, of course, a major fire vintage. Our wines were picked before the Glass fire, but during and after the LNU lightening complex, and while there is no measurable or perceptible smoke, it’s hard not to see them through that prism. The wines seem rich even compared to previous vintages and with enough time open, most seem ready to be enjoyed now. A welcome change from previous releases that require years.

We’ll have full notes and pricing will remain the same as previous releases.’

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
Mike_Wisniewski:

Went back and forth on whether to take first time allocation. Those passing (for pretty compelling reasons) and the smoke year is absolutely noted.

Given that I have not tried yet and secondary availability at release price is moot, I went with 2 Heintz Rose which should be accessible soon enough to make a long-term determination on where this list is headed. May have been covered in the smoke taint thread, as lack of perception of smoke taint now might now does not mean that it won’t appear later on, especially if the sparking wine needs cellar time. What in the world does that mean for Sparkling? Wonder if Schramsberg has any data on it or Oregon sparkling producers?

It’s pretty hard to get smoke taint in white wines because the vast majority of the smoke compounds are in the skins. You can measure smoke in the berries and then in the corresponding wines and you have to have A LOT of smoke exposure for any significant amount of impact to be seen on the wines.
You can measure for smoke marker compounds and if they aren’t there, smoke won’t appear later. In most white wines, they simply aren’t there. I would imagine that in traditional method sparkling, with its very delicate pressing, you would be even less likely to retain any smoke impact. I am not an expert on sparkling wine production but I would be very surprised if these wines were impacted by smoke.

posts.name: Mike Steinberg
posts.cooked:
Just got a response.

HI Mike,

Thank you for reaching out. I spoke to Michael about your email.

First and foremost, we are so very sorry that your first experience with Ultramarine wasn’t an excellent one. To that end, please accept either a credit on your next order or a refund for the bottle of Hirsch Rose.

Regarding smoke, this fruit was picked before the fires but we still had full panels run on it that show no signs of smoke taint in any form. That being said, it was a particular vintage where drought and heat and vine stress seemed to increase a certain green character in all of the wines to varying degrees. Once I received your email, I went home and opened two bottles of the Hirsch Rosé along with a bottle of the Heintz Blanc de blancs. The green note was definitely present but I found it dissipated after about 20-30 min open. These wines need at least 6 more months to settle, and then a bit of time to open and I hope that you find them truly enjoyable experiences then, while still being a product of a particular vintage.

There you have it. I’m hoping that jI just got 1 flawed bottle.

If they held for a year, they would probably charge $5-10 more per bottle because of storage/cellar time. With prices already creeping up, that would hurt them, especially if all or most bottle cross the mental $100/bottle price before shipping.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
A few thoughts on the topics brought up in this thread:

  1. I’ve always found Ultramarine to be variable from wine to wine and vintage to vintage. Green and herbal notes, volatility, brett, and too much oak have appeared in many of the wines since the start. I’ve enjoyed some of the wines and been disappointed in others. I’ve seen others love the wines and others not get them. I say this because what is now starting to be “found” more often in Ultramarine has always been there IMO.

  2. Post-Disgorgement aging can make a huge difference. For me, 6 months is the bare minimum you should wait before opening any Champagne and 12 months is even better. The wine experiences a shock at disgorgement and even without dosage, if wine is added post-disgorgement, you need some time for everything to integrate. Disgorging on the spot (à la volée) is an entirely different situation as you aren’t adding dosage or wine, aren’t recorking the bottle, and are tasting the wine right after disgorgement. A wine can taste quite nice in this method and is not the same thing as disgorging and then preparing the wine for regular release and sale. As for Ultramarine, I would advise waiting a year post-disgorgement before opening. While some wines in some years might be fine a month after disgorgement, most are not going to show at their best. One school of thought is that since Ultramarine is selling/shipping with less than 4 weeks of post-disgorgement age that they must feel the wine is ready. I wish Ultramarine could/wait a year post-disgorgement, but they do have a business to run.

  3. Based on Michael Cruse’s feedback which was posted on this thread and my experience with past Hirsch Rose wines from Ultramarine, I don’t think there is any smoke taint. The Hirsch Rose has shown IMO a spicy, cinnamon note mixed with herbal oak notes. I could see someone calling this out as smoke, ashtray, weird, etc…

  4. I can’t speak to anyone else’s experience with Michael Cruse or Ultramarine. I have never tried emailing him or Ultramarine and don’t know what he may or may not have said publicly. My reviews/thoughts on Ultramarine have not always been positive and I questioned the sudden popularity and rising prices right from the start as I didn’t think the quality and consistency was there to logically support this (I still don’t). Michael Cruse knew this, but we have had nothing but a couple cordial in-person conversations. I’ve found him open minded and friendly although it has been a number of years since I talked to him.

I’m not aware of any smoke-related recalls on impacted wines that made it to market (blessedly few). As you know, smoke impacts aren’t simple, they aren’t black and white, and nor are people’s sensory experiences with them. Like Brett. Do wineries routinely recall wines because of Brett? People make release decisions and let the chips fall.

That said, in sparkling wines a vintage 2019 wine probably went into bottle in 2020 and it’s possible that the green flavors manifested in the secondary fermentation. Not a helpful answer, but the best I have.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Larry,

I think the honest answer for 99% of the time is that an assumotion is made about what brings specific green flavors to wines. The likelihood that the stems in my 2005 ferment contributed to the green bean flavor, in my opinion, is pretty high. But did I do a thorough chemical analysis to sort that out? I did not.

More often than not we’re assigning causation to extremely complex chemical processes with a very high level of speculation.

But looking at pyrazines-they are present in high levels prior to veraison and then diminish as the fruit ripens between 0-20 Brix. Notably sparkling wines are typically picked between 16-19 Brix.

Second, one of the bigger impacts on quantites of pyrazines in grapes is canopy vigor, studies show sunshine on the fruit and stems leads to lower levels of pyrazines. This one is fairly important to Goodfellow wines as we are typically using very high levels of whole cluster and picking quickly.

Again, I have no knowledge of the UM situation. But a look at the 2015 Champagne thread speaks to green flavors in the wines, and in some people’s opinions those green flavors will evolve out with time. While they do not reference jalapeno or other capsaicin flavors in that commentary, it’s reasonable to state that there are different types of green flavors in red and white wines, and that pyrazines might be amplified by stems but they can certainly occur in wines that are pressed off the skins immediately.

posts.name: Ben M a n d l e r
posts.cooked:
Robert_M_yers:

Can you post that again layman’s terms

Pyrazines are a large class of extremely odorous molecules responsible for a whole bunch of smells in plants. When people talk about “pyrazines” in wine they are talking mostly about a type of pyrazine that smells strongly of bell pepper or jalapeño, depending on the other things that are in there with it. The specific molecule that we usually measure in the lab is called isobutylmethoxypyrazine, or IBMP. Some people can smell it at concentrations as low as a few parts per trillion, and in addition to smelling vegetal it strongly masks fruit aromas. It’s an important part of the varietal aroma of Cabernet Sauvignon, Cabernet Franc, and Sauvignon Blanc, and is present to some extent in all Bordeaux varieties and a bunch of others. It accumulates early in the growth of the berry and is degraded as the berry ripens.

Unfortunately, when people say a wine tastes “green”, that can mean a lot of things. It can mean green like bell pepper, it can mean green like grass, green like green beans, or just generally “green” meaning underripe (and probably many other types of green besides). All of those things can be “green” but they have different origins and mean different things about the grape growing and winemaking, making it harder to share ideas about causes of “greenness” in a wine.

Pinot Noir has lower levels of pyrazines that Bordeaux varieties, and when destemmed it’s almost never a significant component of their aroma - at least, I’ve never seen it. But stems do have a lot of pyrazines in them; in theory I could see that making a difference in a whole cluster ferment but wouldn’t expect it to be significant in rose for sparkling, which is presumably not pressed very heavily and does not have a lot of opportunity to extract pyrazines out of the stems. So I’m curious about Marcus’ experience because I’ve never seen it in my own experience and would be very interested to see or hear about an example of pyrazines being a significant aroma in Pinot Noir.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
We don’t measure pyrazines, primarily because they are not an issue for us. That 05 100% whole
Cluster wine was among the only wines I’ve ever made that had such an intense green flavor. And yes, it could have been something else. I have smelled jalapeno in Pinot Noir before, and on multiple occasions, though still at a very low percentage of the overall number of wines I have tasted.

Regarding PN having less, I would have to ask if that is measuring pyrazines at different levels of maturity? Pinot Noir ripens earlier than many varieties, and is often picked well into maturity. But in the WV some producers, including Goodfellow, are picking earlier. Especially as sparkling wines are coming to be a bigger part of what we do. IMO, it’s really important to expose the clusters to the sun and restrict canopy in order to harvest fruit with mature flavors at 16-19 Brix.

We also only reach moderate temperatures on whole cluster ferments, and do not ever use enzymes in order to minimize extracting green vegetal flavors from the stems. That coming from tasting a friends whole cluster wines, where some of the higher levels (above 50%) could have a strongly vegetal component to the aromatics (I’m not saying those were pyrazines though).

I came across a bottle of 2020 Heintz Rosé last night, and honestly, it’s not as bad as some people make it out to be. Yes, there’s a green note, and yes, it’s a bit smoky, but the green character is more akin to the herbal qualities found in Champagnes like Cazé-Thibault, Marguet, and Tim Stroebel. To me, this feels closer to a rosé cava than a rosé Champagne (As I often find cava made with xarel-los to have a similar smokiness). While it might not be worth the full ongoing price, it’s definitely not a drain pour in my opinion. If you are holding on to multiple bottles of 2020s, give it a few months, and I can see this becoming much more enjoyable.

I have never knowingly had an Ultramarine wine (maybe?), but a kind friend offered to bring to dinner last night and I was excited to try. I hadn’t read any notes, so no preconceptions other than it was “good”.

Narrator: It was not “good”.

  • 2020 Ultramarine Blanc de Noirs (Late Disgorged) Charles Heintz Vineyard - USA, California, Sonoma County, Sonoma Coast (2/14/2025)
    A generous friend brought this bottle to a wine dinner to share. It was obvious from the jump that the bottle was off. Pronounced vegetal qualities on the nose and the palate obscured whatever fruit was underneath. While the smell had an asparagus quality, the flavor was a dead ringer for Harvest Snaps -- a dried greeen bean snack food.

    While one person at the table opined that the issue might have had to due with the extendeed time on lees, the notes for the "regular" disgorgment speak of a similar green character. The only obvious flaw to the wine is that it was ever released to the public. If scoring, 60 points.

    While holding might improve the wine some -- indeed the wine was only disgorged 11/24 -- my sense is that you're only wasting cellar space. Genunely awful.

The person who brought this wine lurks on WB, and if he happens to see this note, I want to emphasize that I really appreciated that he shared this with us, even though the result was disappointing. It was far more interesting than if the wine was only ok.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
So, I did open a bottle of the Hirsch Rosé around NYE to see what the discussion was about! On balance I thought I’d leave everyone to it, given on the one hand that many of you have more Ultramarine experience than me, and on the other, that I participate in enough of the wine world’s heated debates as it is. But, you forced my hand…

What I found was quite a pronounced vegetal reduction on opening. So, I decanted half the bottle and left the rest in the bottle, and followed over 24 hours. In the decanted portion, the reduction dissipated and revealed quite an attractive red berry fruit profile, with a gently herbal/minty edge. Just the effect of opening didn’t seem to be enough, because the part that remained in bottle didn’t move all that much.

My analysis was that time will be kind to this wine, even if it’s not going to number among Ultramarine’s greatest hits; and I would strongly recommend a decant (chill your decanter in advance) if you want to check in on it in the near term.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I don’t decant sparkling wines in general, but I was curious to see what happened. Sure enough, it did make a big difference vs. simply leaving the bottle open.

Given that CO2 amplifies the perception of bitterness (which is why Champagne pressing is so gentle, to avoid any bitter elements in the vins clairs), and reduction also amplifies the perception of bitterness, pronounced reduction in sparkling wine can be quite disagreeable in a way it rarely is in still wine. The fragility of most vins clairs to oxidation and the oxidative shock of disgorgement, however, means that actively reduced sparkling wines are rare. So I was curious to try to understand what was going on.
